planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. As the name indicates, Jaune de Deux Saisons produces two crops: a breba crop on old wood in June–July and a main crop on new wood in September–October. The two-crop habit extends the fresh fig season significantly. In zone 9 both crops ripen reliably.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Jaune de Deux Saisons (Yellow of Two Seasons) is a French two-crop fig producing yellow-green to golden-yellow fruits in both the breba (early summer) and main crop (fall) seasons — one of the most productive two-crop fig varieties for home gardens because both crops are of comparable quality and size. The medium-sized, round to slightly pear-shaped fruits have golden-yellow skin and pale amber to pink-tinged flesh with a mild, honeyed sweetness and delicate flavor. The yellow color is distinctive and immediately identifiable among darker-skinned varieties. Two-crop figs like Jaune de Deux Saisons are particularly valuable in zone 9 where the long warm season allows both crops to mature fully — the breba crop in June provides early-season figs while the main crop extends harvest into October. The golden color makes this fig outstanding for fresh presentations and pale fig jam.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Yellow Fig Cultivars
University of California Cooperative Extension — Two-Crop Fig Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Fig Variety Selection

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in early spring before bud break; in zone 9 plant February–March. Mango is a named cultivar from the USDA breeding program at Beltsville that produces distinctly mango-like fruit flavor. Requires a second pawpaw variety for cross-pollination; use another named cultivar within 50 feet. Avoid disturbing the fragile taproot at transplanting.

directions: Plant in partial shade when young, transitioning to full sun as the tree matures; Mango Pawpaw is a named USDA-selected cultivar known for producing fruits with a distinctly tropical, mango-like flavor quality that is more pronounced than in typical seedling pawpaws — a richer, more aromatic, tropical sweetness beyond the standard banana-vanilla pawpaw flavor. The medium to large fruits weigh 6–12 ounces with bright yellow flesh and a creamy, custard-like texture. Mango is considered one of the finest-flavored named pawpaw cultivars in the Corwin Davis breeding tradition and is widely used in taste trials as a benchmark for outstanding pawpaw flavor. Like all pawpaw cultivars, Mango requires cross-pollination from a different named cultivar — plant alongside Overleese, Wells, or NC-1 for reliable fruit set. Ripe fruits fall from the tree in September–October and must be refrigerated or eaten within a few days; the short shelf life is inherent to the species.
sources: Kentucky State University Pawpaw Program — Mango Cultivar Profile
Ohio State University Extension — Named Pawpaw Varieties
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Asimina triloba Breeding Program

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in early spring before bud break; in zone 9 plant February–March. NC-1 is a very cold-hardy cultivar selected in Canada and also one of the most productive named pawpaw varieties — a combination that makes it the top choice when heavy production and flavor quality are both priorities. Requires cross-pollination from a second variety for maximum fruit set.

directions: Plant in partial shade when young, transitioning to full sun as the tree matures; NC-1 is a named pawpaw cultivar selected in Ontario, Canada by Richard Campbell and subsequently widely adopted in North American pawpaw cultivation for combining two usually separate virtues: exceptional cold hardiness (rated to USDA zone 4) and superior fruiting productivity in the upper part of its range. The medium-large fruits (8–14 ounces) have cream-to-yellow flesh with a rich, complex, sweet flavor rated highly in Kentucky State University pawpaw taste evaluations. NC-1 is noted for its consistent, reliable fruiting on young trees — often beginning to bear meaningfully in 3–4 years from transplant, ahead of the 5–7 year wait common for seedling pawpaws. Its northern Canadian selection history means it leafs out slightly later in spring, reducing the risk of late frost damage to flowers — a significant advantage for zone 9 gardeners where erratic late-winter weather can damage early flowers. Cross-pollinate with Mango, Overleese, or Shenandoah for best results.
sources: Kentucky State University Pawpaw Program — NC-1 Cultivar Profile
Ontario Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs — Pawpaw Production
Ohio State University Extension — NC-1 Pawpaw

planting.note: Plant dormant cuttings or container-grown vines in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Ison is a perfect-flowered (self-fertile) muscadine that does not require a pollinator vine. Train on a sturdy trellis or pergola; the vigorous vines can spread 20–30 feet. In zone 9 Ison ripens in late August–September. Prune heavily in late winter to encourage productive new growth.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Ison is one of the most widely planted muscadine varieties in the Southeast, selected by Ison's Nursery in Georgia for its combination of very large, sweet, black-bronze-skinned fruits and self-fertility — the perfect-flowered habit means a single Ison vine sets full crops without requiring a separate pollinator vine. The large, round to slightly oval fruits ripen to deep bronze-black with a rich, sweet, complex muscadine flavor and relatively low tannin compared to older varieties. Ison is used for fresh eating, juice, wine, and muscadine jelly, where the large fruit size and good juice content are advantageous. The vine is vigorous and productive once established, with crops of 30–60 pounds per mature vine in good growing conditions. Unlike some muscadine varieties that have a thick, tough skin, Ison's skin is more tender and the slip-skin characteristic less pronounced, making fresh eating more pleasant.
sources: Ison's Nursery — Ison Muscadine Cultivar Notes
University of Georgia Extension — Muscadine Grape Varieties for Home Gardens
North Carolina State University Extension — Muscadine Grape Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Plant dormant cuttings or container-grown vines in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Triumph is a perfect-flowered (self-fertile) muscadine that sets good crops without a pollinator but benefits from cross-pollination. In zone 9 it ripens in late August–September. Train on a sturdy trellis; the vigorous vines require heavy winter pruning to produce the next season's crop.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Triumph is a bronze-skinned (white) muscadine grape selected by the University of Georgia for large, sweet, bronze-gold fruits with an exceptionally sweet flavor and low acidity — one of the sweetest muscadine varieties available and a benchmark for fresh-eating quality among bronze muscadines. The large, round fruits (1–1.25 inches) ripen to a deep bronze-gold color and have a thin, tender skin with less of the tough slip-skin character that makes some muscadine varieties less appealing for fresh eating. The sweet, mild, slightly floral flavor with classic muscadine aromatic notes makes Triumph outstanding for fresh eating, and the golden color produces beautiful pale-gold juice and jelly. Triumph is a self-fertile (perfect-flowered) variety that sets crops reliably without a pollinator. In zone 9 the vine is vigorous and productive; provide generous spacing (20+ feet) for the mature vine's spread.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Triumph Muscadine Variety
North Carolina State University Extension — Bronze Muscadine Cultivars
Mississippi State University Extension — Muscadine Grape Production
